petitioner_background: The petitioner is an economist and political scientist from Kazakhstan who sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A. He received a master's degree in international economics through a Kazakhstan presidential scholarship program that selected students to study abroad and return to Kazakhstan with specialized knowledge. He was employed as an economics specialist and intends to continue working in the United States as an economist and political scientist.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his receipt of a Kazakhstan medal (name redacted) established by Kazakhstan Presidential decree to commemorate the 20th anniversary of Kazakhstan's independence and of the Tenge (Kazakhstan's currency). The decree authorizes the chairman and other authorized persons to award the medal on behalf of the President of Kazakhstan to citizens and foreigners 'who have made a significant contribution to the functioning of the national currency' of Kazakhstan. The petitioner also submitted news articles naming notable recipients of the medal, and evidence of other awards, most of which appear to have been academic awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director had found the medal did not meet the criterion because the petitioner 'did not submit major trade publications or other major media conveying that the medal is nationally or internationally recognized for excellence in the field of endeavor (political science),' and suggested the medal may have been given due to the petitioner's employment status rather than for excellence in the field. The AAO disagreed, finding the articles and evidence did not suggest employment status was the reason for receipt; rather, the articles named notable recipients and only generally mentioned some recipients were employees of a particular organization. The AAO agreed with the petitioner that the Kazakhstan Presidential decree establishing the medal conveyed national recognition for significant contributions in economics and political science. The AAO noted the decree, along with articles, explained the significance of Kazakhstan's currency to its economy and political independence, and the importance of the award recognizing those who made significant contributions to the development and strengthening of Kazakhstan's sovereignty. The AAO withdrew the Director's finding and held the petitioner met this criterion by a preponderance of evidence. Other academic awards would be considered in the final merits determination.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an article published in Zhas Alash (a Kazakhstan publication), which was an interview with the petitioner focusing on his participation in a new academic scholarship program initiated by Kazakhstan's President after independence from the U.S.S.R. The article described the petitioner earning a presidential scholarship and degrees in economics and international economics, his employment as an economics specialist, and his perspective on economic issues facing Kazakhstan including market relations, privatization, self-financing, and private ownership. The petitioner also submitted a Wikipedia summary of Zhas Alash showing it was founded in 1921, continued as an independent socio-political newspaper published twice a week, received an award in 2000, and had a circulation of 50,000.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found the article was not about the petitioner's work as a political scientist and that the evidence was insufficient to demonstrate the publication's national readership at the time of publication in 1996. The AAO disagreed, finding the article describes the petitioner's educational successes including earning a presidential scholarship and degrees in economics and international economics, and that the article provides the petitioner's perspective on economic issues of Kazakhstan including his quote: 'Market relations, privatization, self-financing, private ownership rather than state ownership is necessary to understand that the growth of private property is not just another campaign, as in the former socialist society, but a necessity of real life, a reality of development.' The AAO concluded the article is about the petitioner and relates to his work in the fields of political science and economics. Regarding the publication's status, the AAO found the Wikipedia evidence showing 90+ years of existence, twice-weekly publication, an award in 2000, and circulation of 50,000 demonstrated Zhas Alash was a major trade or professional publication at the time the article was published in 1996. The AAO withdrew the Director's finding.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted reports published in conference materials, his book (title redacted), and articles he wrote for magazines, journals, and other publications. Specifically, he submitted a report presented at the 11 International Conference organized by the Republican State Enterprise Institute for Economic Research of the Ministry of Economy and Budget Planning of the Republic of Kazakhstan. The conference published a collection of presenters' reports including the petitioner's report. The conference materials included an introductory summary stating the conference's purpose was to analyze the 'international practice of infrastructure development by the private sector,' that attendees included scientists, specialists, and employees in banking and economic industries, and that materials were intended for 'economists and professionals interested in the development of public and private partnerships for the creation of infrastructure facilities in Kazakhstan.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found the petitioner did not provide evidence that the publications or sources are professional or major trade publications or other major media, and found the reports and articles 'do not appear to be scholarly or in the field of political science.' The AAO explained that this criterion contains multiple evidentiary elements: first, authorship of scholarly articles in the field (which may be within the academic arena reporting on original research, experimentation, or philosophical discourse by researchers affiliated with colleges/universities/research institutions, OR outside the academic arena if written for learned persons defined as 'having or demonstrating profound knowledge or scholarship'); and second, that the articles appear in a professional publication, major trade publication, or major media (requiring evidence of circulation statistics high relative to similar publications and intended audience). The AAO found the petitioner's report and the conference material summary indicated the published material was intended for learned persons in the fields of economics and political science, and that the collection of conference material qualified as professional trade material since it was provided to scientists, specialists, and employees focused on economics and political science. The AAO held the record demonstrated the petitioner met the basic parameters of this criterion and withdrew the Director's finding.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. Upon de novo review, the AAO found the petitioner satisfied three regulatory criteria: lesser awards under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i), published material under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii), and scholarly articles under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi). Having met the initial evidence requirements of at least three criteria, the AAO found it unnecessary to discuss the remaining claimed criteria (published material about him, original contribution, leading/critical role; the petitioner withdrew his commercial successes claim on appeal). On remand, the Director was instructed to conduct a final merits review evaluating the totality of the evidence to determine whether the petitioner demonstrated sustained national or international acclaim and status as one of the small percentage at the very top of his field.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
